other_observers: Were joined later by Nic Korte (president, Grand Valley Audubon Association) who briefly observed birds at end of viewing time.; Yes. Birds observed by myself and Dan (John) Brooke. I first heard them, then we both saw them, Dan exclusively photographed them, inculding for a brief (about 15 min) period of time when I left the site to meet Nic Korte.
specific_location: Birds first seen approximately 0.38 miles up the No Thoroughfare Canyon trail, accessed by the Devil's Kitchen Trailhead parking lot in Colorado National Monument. During approximately one hour and forty minute observation, moved to approximately 0.46 miles up the trail, as measured on Omron pedometer (which was previously fairly closely calibrated on measured walking course by Cheryl Teuton).
description: First encounter was "buzzing" insect like call as above. Then, while we were trying to figure out what made the noise emanating from nearby Juniper, three Black-Chinned Sparrows (BCSP), 2 adult and a slightly stripey chested fledgling popped up. In ensuing hour and a half, watched, photographed and audiotaped 2 adult BCSP feeding a juvenile. Adult BCSP showed gray bodies with brown, streaky backs, black on faces, and pink bills, with dark orangish legs. On review of photographs, one bird that appeared to us to be female had black confined to chin, while apparent male's black appeared to extend above the bill. Tails long and dark. Juvenile gray bodied with vague stripes on chest. No black on face, left bird with "blank" look. Very orange around periphery of open mouth on this bird. Numerous photos taken, selected sent, but more available.Behaviors: Occasionally (three times observed), one bird (presumed male), came to top of Juniper or Pinyon and gave softer version of typical song, longer introduction, with quickening, "bouncing ball" sound at end. We had heard territorial singing by this species last year near this location, this song less than half volume, and much less frequent. Adult birds observed gathering food, then frequently feeding the young. Begging sound was very buzzy, insect-like, and was heard given by all three birds, based on number of calls and volume. Recorded (low volume) by cell phone app (tape-a-talk). We only observed and photographed one fledgling that we are certain about. If there were more, we did not detect them.Call: Heard one adult give softer version of typical "bouncing ball" call 3 times, watched it call once. Buzzing calls between all three birds were our first alert to their presence.Plumage: Breeding (2) and Juvenile (1)
